content.problemStatement:
Chef is working at his dream workplace with N−1N-1N−1 of his friends! Like any dream workplace, this one also has dream work conditions. To ensure absolute happiness of the employees, there are the following rules at the company:


Each worker works on exactly one day! You heard it right: no more, no less.
On each day, at most one worker may work. This provides the perfect work conditions: no crowd, just the work and you.
Every worker has a deadline; let's denote the deadline of the iii-th worker by did_idi​. (This means that the day on which the iii-th worker works must not be later than day did_idi​; the days are numbered starting from 111.) You thought there weren't any limitations, eh?
However, sooner or later, all dream workplaces have to fall. The CLO (Chefland Labour Office) demands that there should be exactly CCC ways to schedule the work such that the above conditions are fulfilled. Two schedulings are different if there is an employee that works at different days in these schedulings.

Chef is asking for your help, since he can't spare any workdays at this company. You should find a sequence of employees' deadlines d1≤d2≤⋯≤dNd_1 \le d_2 \le \dots \le d_Nd1​≤d2​≤⋯≤dN​ such that there are exactly CCC ways to schedule the work. If there are multiple sequences satisfying this condition, do another little favour for Chef: minimise dNd_NdN​ (his deadline).
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and C.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing N space-separated integers d1, d2, …, dN that describe your chosen sequence.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Each worker can work on any day from day 1 to day 4. The 12 possible schedulings (pairs of days on which they work) are [1,2], [1,3], [1,4], [2,3], [2,4], [3,4], [2,1], [3,1], [4,1], [3,2], [4,2], [4,3].

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a greedy algorithm. We start by initializing an array of size N with all elements as 1. Then, we calculate the factorial of N and compare it with C. If the factorial is less than C, we increment the last element of the array and recalculate the factorial until it is greater than or equal to C. We repeat this process for the second last element, third last element, and so on, until we have used up all the remaining value of C.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) — because we are potentially iterating over all N elements in the worst case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — because we need to store the array of size N
